summary: Paul organizes relief for Jerusalem, outlines travel plans, commends faithful coworkers, and closes with courage, love, and grace.

outline.title: The Collection for Jerusalem
outline.summary: Paul gives practical instructions for regular, accountable generosity toward believers in need.

outline.title: Plans for Paul, Timothy, and Apollos
outline.summary: Paul explains changing travel plans and urges the Corinthians to welcome fellow workers without rivalry.

outline.title: Stand Firm and Honor Faithful Workers
outline.summary: The church must combine courage and love while recognizing servants who refresh and strengthen God’s people.

outline.title: Final Greetings and Grace
outline.summary: Paul closes with greetings, a call to love Christ, and blessings of grace and affection.

verses.bibleText: Now concerning the collection for the saints, as I have given order to the churches of Galatia, even so do ye.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
After the majestic teaching about resurrection, Paul turns immediately to a practical matter: “the collection for the saints.” Eternal hope does not remove believers from present human need. Because Christ has been raised and the labor of His people is not in vain, ordinary acts of generosity can become part of God’s enduring work. The faith that looks forward to resurrection also opens its hands to suffering brothers and sisters now.

The word “saints” refers to people who belong to God through Christ. Their need does not diminish their dignity. They are not presented as anonymous objects of pity but as members of the same spiritual family. The Corinthians may be separated from them geographically, yet shared belonging to Christ creates a responsibility that reaches beyond the boundaries of one congregation.

Paul had given the churches of Galatia the same instruction. This shows that the collection was not an isolated appeal made only to Corinth. Several congregations were being invited to participate in a common ministry of care. Such giving could strengthen unity among believers from different places and backgrounds. The churches were learning that Christianity was larger than their local concerns. To belong to Christ was also to belong to His people.

The reference to instructions previously given elsewhere also reveals consistency in Paul’s leadership. He is not inventing a special rule for one troubled church. The same principles of generosity and stewardship are being taught throughout the wider fellowship.

The wider correspondence identifies the destination as needy believers in Jerusalem. This relief project joined Gentile congregations to Jewish Christians in a visible confession of one body. The collection was therefore both material mercy and gospel reconciliation, but it was never a payment for spiritual status or a means of purchasing favor from God.

wordNotes.explanation: This was a special relief offering for needy believers in Jerusalem, not a general rule about tithes. Calling the recipients “saints” preserves their dignity as members of God’s family. Gentile congregations expressed material care and the unity created by the gospel.

verses.bibleText: Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him, that there be no gatherings when I come.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The subject is the Jerusalem relief collection. Paul asks every Corinthian believer to set aside an amount on the first day of each week, in proportion to how he or she has prospered, so that no hurried fundraising will be needed when he arrives. The verse establishes an orderly saving practice; it does not legislate a new day of worship.

“Let every one of you lay by him in store” translates par’ heautō, language that naturally means setting something aside by oneself or at home. The most straightforward picture is personal or household saving until the appointed transfer. Even if the phrase were taken to mean deposits into a common fund, it would still describe financial administration for relief. Handling money on a recurring day does not make that day sacred.

The sentence does not describe a worship service or congregational gathering. It mentions no sermon, Communion, prayer service, cessation from labor, blessing of the day, or command to keep it holy. Paul does not call the first day a Sabbath or a memorial of the resurrection. The text supplies neither an abolition of the seventh-day Sabbath nor a transfer of the fourth commandment to Sunday. A doctrine of Sunday sacredness cannot be drawn from details the verse never states.

This conclusion does not require claiming that Christians could never meet on the first day. Believers may gather on any day for worship, fellowship, or service. The question is narrower: does 1 Corinthians 16:2 assign holiness to Sunday or replace the creation Sabbath? It does not. The resurrection is central to the gospel and is confessed in baptism and the Lord’s Supper, but Paul does not turn this collection schedule into a new weekly holy day.

The positive instruction is rich. Giving is regular rather than impulsive, personal rather than delegated to a few patrons, and proportionate rather than identical. “As God hath prospered him” allows those with more to give more without shaming those whose means are small. Advance preparation also protects the church from emotional pressure when Paul arrives.

What may look like household bookkeeping becomes disciplined mercy. Each week believers review God’s provision and reserve part of it for suffering saints whom they may never have met. The first day marks a practical rhythm of generosity; the sanctity of the seventh-day Sabbath rests where Scripture places it—in creation and God’s command.

wordNotes.term: Lay by him — παρ’ ἑαυτῷ (par’ heautō)
wordNotes.explanation: An idiom meaning to set something aside by oneself. In context it describes each person’s advance preparation of a relief gift, naturally at the personal or household level.

verses.bibleText: And when I come, whomsoever ye shall approve by your letters, them will I send to bring your liberality unto Jerusalem.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The congregation, rather than Paul acting alone, will approve the people entrusted with its gift. The Greek permits two ways of connecting the phrase about letters. It may mean that the Corinthians will identify their delegates by letters, as the King James Version reads, or that Paul will send the approved delegates with letters of introduction. The wording does not settle who writes them. Either way, recognized representatives will carry the offering openly to Jerusalem.

That arrangement protects the character of the collection. Paul organized and encouraged the relief effort, but the money never becomes his private possession. The Corinthians choose trustworthy carriers, written communication explains their commission, and the receiving church can recognize whom they represent. The delegates do not appear as self-appointed claimants with an unexplained sum.

Apostolic authority therefore operates alongside transparent administration. Paul does not ask the church to suspend ordinary safeguards because it trusts his motives. Shared responsibility protects the gift, its handlers, and the reputation of the gospel. It also allows the offering to remain the visible love of a congregation rather than the display of one celebrated benefactor. Many believers saved toward it; their approved messengers now embody that fellowship.

Generosity and careful procedure belong together. Love supplies the gift, while accountable stewardship helps it reach the intended people without secrecy, confusion, or personal control. Paul leaves the grammatical detail about the letters open, but his pastoral principle is unmistakable: sacred purposes do not excuse careless handling of money.

wordNotes.explanation: The congregation would choose trustworthy delegates to carry its gift. The Greek leaves open whether Corinth supplied their letters or Paul sent them with letters of introduction. In either reading, shared approval and written authorization made the transfer accountable rather than dependent on one leader’s private control.

verses.bibleText: And if it be meet that I go also, they shall go with me.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul leaves open the possibility that he may accompany the approved representatives to Jerusalem. “If it seems advisable” indicates careful judgment rather than an assertion that his presence must dominate the mission. He does not assume that every important work requires his personal control.

The appropriateness of his participation would be determined by the circumstances. Paul is willing to go if that will serve the purpose of the collection, but the gift does not depend upon him. The congregational delegates are capable of carrying out the responsibility entrusted to them. His apostleship does not reduce everyone else to passive assistants.

If Paul does travel, “they will accompany” him. The wording preserves the representatives’ dignity. They are not merely Paul’s attendants, and he is not replacing them as the accountable carriers of the gift. The collection remains a shared ministry in which the congregation’s chosen people have an identifiable role. This arrangement further strengthens financial transparency. Paul will not travel alone with the money. Nor will he privately transfer it in a way that leaves the churches uncertain about what happened. The presence of several approved servants creates mutual accountability.

There is also a beautiful picture of fellowship in the possibility of the journey. The gift is not simply money sent from one place to another. Human representatives carry the love, concern, and prayers of the churches. Their presence can communicate something no material contribution by itself can express: believers far away know of the recipients’ need and regard them as family.

Christian stewardship is strongest when generosity, accountability, and cooperation remain together. The church gives, approved servants carry, Paul may accompany them, and all serve the same purpose. No one needs to possess the work, because the gift ultimately expresses the grace of God moving through His people toward others in need.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul leaves his own participation to what proves fitting rather than making himself indispensable. The approved delegates remain central, and he may travel with them. The arrangement combines apostolic involvement with congregational ownership and accountability.

verses.bibleText: Now I will come unto you, when I shall pass through Macedonia: for I do pass through Macedonia.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now turns from the collection to his travel plans. He intends to come to Corinth after passing through Macedonia. The statement is practical, but it also reveals something about the connected life of the churches. Paul’s ministry is not centered upon one congregation alone. He moves among communities, strengthening relationships and serving the wider mission entrusted to him.

His plan to pass through Macedonia does not imply indifference toward Corinth. The following verses will show that he wants to spend meaningful time with the Corinthians rather than make a hurried stop. Macedonia is part of the route he expects to take, while Corinth is a place where he hopes to remain longer. After everything Paul has addressed in the letter—divisions, moral disorder, lawsuits, marriage, Christian freedom, worship, spiritual gifts, and resurrection—he still desires to see them. Correction has not ended the relationship. Their serious problems have not caused him to abandon them as hopeless. He plans to move toward them.

This reflects the character of pastoral love. It is easier to write about problems from a distance than to enter the difficult work of restored fellowship. Paul intends to do both. The letter speaks truth plainly, while the planned visit expresses his continuing commitment to the people receiving that truth.

His announcement also allows the Corinthians to prepare. Paul does not appear without warning and then demand that they reorganize everything around him. He communicates his intentions openly. Responsible planning honors those whom a servant hopes to visit. Yet this is a plan rather than an unconditional prophecy about his movements. Paul can identify the direction he expects to take while remaining open to God’s guidance. Human foresight has a legitimate place in Christian service, but it is never sovereign.

His path through Macedonia toward Corinth belongs to a larger life of service. Geography, travel, relationships, and practical decisions all become part of ministry when they are directed toward the strengthening of God’s people. Paul’s plans are not detached logistics. They are expressions of love seeking an opportunity to become personally present.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul sketches an intended route through the northern Aegean churches before reaching Corinth. His plans show sustained pastoral coordination across several congregations, while later correspondence reveals that circumstances could alter the timing without making his ministry faithless.

verses.bibleText: And it may be that I will abide, yea, and winter with you, that ye may bring me on my journey whithersoever I go.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul hopes that he may stay with the Corinthians for some time, perhaps even for an extended season. He does not yet know the exact length, but his desire is clear: he wants more than a brief appearance. He wishes to share life with them, continue ministry among them, and allow the strained relationship to be strengthened through personal presence.

This congregation had caused Paul considerable concern, yet he did not regard them merely as a problem to be managed. He wanted fellowship. Letters could instruct, warn, and encourage, but some forms of pastoral care require time together. Questions need conversation, wounds need patient attention, and trust often grows slowly.

Paul also expects that the Corinthians may “send” him on his next journey. Such sending could include prayer, encouragement, hospitality, practical supplies, companionship, or other forms of assistance suited to the journey. He is not ashamed to acknowledge that ministry is supported through the cooperation of believers.

This does not contradict his earlier refusal to receive certain forms of support from them. Paul could distinguish between exploiting a congregation and accepting appropriate assistance freely offered for continuing gospel work. The needs of ministry are real, and helping a faithful worker proceed can become a meaningful participation in that work. The words “wherever I may go” show that some details remain unsettled. Paul has a direction in mind, but not every step has been finalized. He communicates what he knows without pretending to know more.

His possible extended stay also reveals that difficult churches should not always be given less attention. Sometimes the presence of unresolved problems makes sustained care more necessary, not less. Paul does not plan to rush through Corinth, issue commands, collect assistance, and disappear. He wants to remain long enough for ministry to become relational.

Faithful Christian service makes responsible plans while leaving room for providence, receives help without turning people into resources, and values time with believers as more than a means to another destination. Paul desires to be with the Corinthians because, despite their failures, they remain loved members of Christ’s church.

wordNotes.explanation: The phrase describes practical missionary hospitality: lodging, supplies, companions, prayer, and assistance for the next stage. Paul hopes for more than a brief appearance; a winter stay would allow substantial time with the Corinthians and make them partners in the work beyond their city.

verses.bibleText: For I will not see you now by the way; but I trust to tarry a while with you, if the Lord permit.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul explains why he does not want to visit Corinth immediately while merely passing through. A short encounter would not satisfy the need he perceives. He hopes to spend a meaningful period with them, “if the Lord permits.”

The congregation’s difficulties required more than a ceremonial appearance. Paul had addressed matters touching nearly every part of their shared life. Some would need repentance; others would need encouragement, instruction, or reconciliation. A hurried visit might allow everyone to exchange greetings without entering the deeper work of restoration.

Yet his plan remains subject to the Lord. “If the Lord permits” is neither empty religious language nor a refusal to plan. Paul has already stated what he intends to do. He is responsible enough to think ahead, but humble enough to recognize that circumstances and opportunities remain under God’s authority. Submission to God’s will does not mean passivity. Paul does not sit still waiting for every detail to be revealed before making any decision. He considers the needs of the churches, chooses a route, communicates the plan, and entrusts its fulfillment to the Lord.

This posture guards against both anxiety and presumption. Paul need not control the future, and he need not pretend that his desires are identical with God’s decisions. He can deeply hope for the visit while remaining willing to be redirected.

There is pastoral tenderness in the phrase “spend some time with you.” Paul wants the Corinthians, not merely the successful completion of a task. Their church is not an item on a schedule. They are people whose faith, unity, holiness, and hope matter to him. Christian ministry can become hurried and impersonal when people are treated as appointments rather than souls. Paul’s plan challenges that spirit. Some work cannot be accomplished through a passing encounter. Faithful presence, listening, teaching, and shared life require time.

He therefore delays a brief visit in hope of a fuller one. The delay should not be read as indifference. It arises from his desire to serve them more completely—while remembering that even the best pastoral intention must remain beneath the wisdom and permission of the Lord.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul makes real plans while acknowledging that their fulfillment remains under Christ’s authority. This is neither fatalism nor a pious formula excusing poor preparation. Christian planning holds intention and humility together, ready to obey when providence redirects the route.

verses.bibleText: But I will tarry at Ephesus until Pentecost.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
For the present, Paul intends to remain in Ephesus until Pentecost. The festival serves as a recognizable point in his schedule, allowing the Corinthians to understand that his departure is not immediate. He has chosen to stay because the work before him still requires attention.

Remaining can demand as much discernment as moving. Missionary eagerness might make constant travel appear more spiritual, but Paul does not leave simply because another congregation needs him. He weighs the present opportunity and concludes that he should continue where he is for a time. His decision also shows that concern for Corinth does not cancel other responsibilities. Paul loves them and hopes to visit, yet he is accountable to a wider calling. Healthy ministry resists the assumption that the loudest need must always become the only need.

His continued stay in Ephesus will be explained in the next verse. There is a significant opportunity for gospel work, even though opposition is also present. Paul’s schedule is therefore shaped not by convenience alone but by spiritual responsibility.

There are seasons when faithfulness means going, and seasons when it means staying. Neither movement nor stillness is automatically proof of obedience. The question is where God has presently opened a meaningful field of service and what responsibilities have been entrusted. Paul waits in Ephesus not because Corinth is unimportant, but because the Lord’s work cannot be reduced to one place. He trusts that God can care for the Corinthians while he remains faithful to the opportunity presently before him.

wordNotes.explanation: Pentecost gives Paul a recognizable calendar marker for his intended departure from Ephesus. Mentioning the feast does not by itself establish where or how he would observe it; the verse chiefly locates his travel plan within the season following Passover.

verses.bibleText: For a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul describes Ephesus through a striking combination: “a great and effective door” has opened, and “there are many adversaries.” Opportunity and opposition exist together. The presence of resistance does not persuade him that the opportunity is unreal. The open door is an opportunity for gospel ministry. People can be reached, truth can be proclaimed, and the work can bear fruit. Paul does not claim that he created the opening through superior strategy. The image suggests access that has become available and that should not be neglected.

Ephesus was a major center of Roman Asia, and devotion to Artemis shaped its civic pride and commerce. The conflict recorded during Paul’s ministry there involved both religious allegiance and the livelihood of artisans connected with the sanctuary trade. His “open door” was therefore concrete: the gospel was reaching people in a city whose public identity and economy could make resistance costly.

The door is not merely wide; it is effective. Something meaningful is happening through the ministry. This is why Paul remains. He is not staying because Ephesus is comfortable or because his reputation is secure. He sees an opportunity in which his labor can genuinely serve the advance of Christ’s message.

This guards believers from simplistic conclusions about guidance. Difficulty does not always mean God has closed a door. Sometimes opposition arises precisely where the gospel is making an impact. Yet resistance by itself does not prove that a ministry is divinely appointed. People may face criticism because they have acted unwisely or wrongly. The reality of an open door must be discerned through faithfulness to Christ, the truth of the message, spiritual fruit, and responsible judgment.

Likewise, an opportunity without opposition is not automatically God’s will. Ease can be attractive without being faithful. Paul does not measure guidance merely by comfort or discomfort.

His decision to remain shows courage without recklessness. He does not seek adversaries or glorify danger. He stays because the opportunity outweighs the pressure and because he believes his presence is presently useful. If protection, withdrawal, or another course became necessary, faithfulness could take a different form.

wordNotes.explanation: The open door is a substantial opportunity for fruitful gospel work in Ephesus. Its presence alongside “many adversaries” shows that divine opportunity is not measured by ease or lack of conflict. Opposition may accompany, rather than disprove, an effective ministry opening.

verses.bibleText: Now if Timotheus come, see that he may be with you without fear: for he worketh the work of the Lord, as I also do.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s concern turns to Timothy: “When Timothy comes, see that he has nothing to fear among you.” The instruction does not brand Timothy as cowardly or spiritually weak. It places responsibility upon the congregation to receive him in a manner that does not intimidate, humiliate, or threaten him.

Corinth could be a demanding environment for a Christian worker. Throughout the letter, some members have shown strong opinions, pride in status, and readiness to judge leaders by outward impressiveness. Timothy must not be treated as though he has to prove his worth through forceful personality, social standing, or polished performance.

The reason for receiving him respectfully is simple: “he is doing the work of the Lord, as I am.” Timothy’s dignity rests in the work entrusted to him. Paul does not say that Timothy must imitate every feature of Paul’s personality. They share a calling to serve the Lord, even though they are distinct people. The phrase “as I am” places Timothy’s ministry beside Paul’s without turning either man into the center. Both belong to the Lord, and both serve His work. The Corinthians therefore cannot honor Paul while mistreating the coworker he sends.

A congregation’s treatment of less prominent workers reveals much about its values. It is easy to welcome someone famous, confident, or powerful. Christian hospitality becomes more searching when the visitor appears ordinary, reserved, young, or lacking the qualities a culture naturally admires. The church must evaluate servants by faithfulness to Christ rather than by worldly measurements.

Creating a setting “without fear” means more than preventing physical harm. It includes refusing contempt, hostile interrogation, ridicule, and unnecessary suspicion. Faithful workers should be able to serve without constantly defending their right to be present. This does not remove accountability. Timothy’s work can still be considered in the light of the gospel. Respect is not blind acceptance of every claim. Yet examination must not become cruelty, especially when the servant has already been commended as one doing the Lord’s work.

wordNotes.explanation: Timothy’s legitimate ministry does not guarantee an easy reception in status-conscious Corinth. Paul asks the church to create conditions in which the younger coworker can serve without intimidation. Protecting faithful workers from contempt is part of sharing in the work of the Lord.

verses.bibleText: Let no man therefore despise him: but conduct him forth in peace, that he may come unto me: for I look for him with the brethren.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“No one, then, should treat him with contempt.” Paul moves from Timothy’s possible fear to the congregation’s conduct. Disdain can silence, isolate, and deeply wound a faithful worker. The Corinthians must not assume that Timothy’s authority is negligible because he differs from the leaders they find impressive.

Contempt may appear through mockery, dismissal, refusal to listen, or the quiet decision that someone’s words need not be taken seriously. Paul forbids such treatment. Timothy does the Lord’s work; therefore, honoring him is part of honoring the work Christ has entrusted to him. The congregation is also to “send him on his way in peace.” Their responsibility does not end with allowing him to visit. They are to help him leave safely and honorably, supplying the encouragement and practical assistance appropriate to his continuing journey.

Peace here includes more than the absence of open conflict. Timothy should not leave carrying the injuries of humiliation, unresolved hostility, or deliberate neglect. The Corinthians are to treat him as a brother whose service matters and whose well-being deserves care.

Paul is waiting for Timothy “with the brothers.” Timothy belongs to a wider circle of ministry and fellowship. His visit to Corinth is not an isolated private venture. How the Corinthians receive him will affect relationships beyond their own congregation. This reminds the church that local behavior has wider consequences. A congregation does not have the right to mistreat a worker simply because it considers itself independent. Believers belong to one body, and those sent between churches should be received in a manner consistent with that unity.

Paul does not tell Timothy merely to become tougher. He addresses the community that could create the problem. Spiritual maturity is not proved by how much mistreatment a worker can endure. Sometimes the needed correction belongs to those who have made service unnecessarily fearful.

By sending Timothy onward in peace, the Corinthians can demonstrate that Paul’s teaching about love has reached their conduct. They can replace intimidation with honor, contempt with recognition, and isolation with fellowship. In doing so, they help sustain the Lord’s work through one of His faithful servants.

wordNotes.explanation: The Corinthians must not measure Timothy by age, rhetoric, or local prestige. They are to receive his service and help him continue his journey in peace. Respect belongs to the Lord’s work he performs, not to a cultivated celebrity status.

verses.bibleText: As touching our brother Apollos, I greatly desired him to come unto you with the brethren: but his will was not at all to come at this time; but he will come when he shall have convenient time.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now addresses Apollos, whose name had earlier been misused by factions in Corinth. Some had said, “I follow Apollos,” while others attached themselves to Paul or other leaders. Against that divisive background, Paul’s words are especially significant: he strongly urged Apollos to visit Corinth. There is no hint of jealousy. Paul does not fear that Apollos will gain influence or strengthen a competing group. He sees him as “our brother” and wants the Corinthians to benefit from his ministry. The rivalry existed in the Corinthians’ use of their names, not necessarily in the relationship between the two workers.

Apollos, however, did not believe the present time was right. Paul respects that judgment. Strong urging did not become coercion. Apostolic authority was not used to erase another servant’s freedom, conscience, or discernment.

The statement that it was “not at all his will to come now” should not be read as rebellion against Paul. Apollos intended to come “when he had opportunity.” His decision concerned timing, not rejection of the Corinthians or hostility toward Paul. The verse gives no basis for inventing a private quarrel. This is a healthy picture of shared ministry. Paul can encourage strongly without controlling. Apollos can decline for the present without breaking fellowship. Both remain committed to the Lord’s work, and neither must dominate the other.

The Corinthians also learn that a respected worker is not available simply because a congregation desires him. Apollos is not a possession of the group that once used his name. Christian leaders belong to Christ and must follow His guidance rather than the demands of admirers.

Paul’s openness about the matter further dismantles factional thinking. Had he viewed Apollos as a rival, he might have prevented the visit or spoken suspiciously about his absence. Instead, he explains that he wanted Apollos to come and that Apollos will do so when circumstances permit.

wordNotes.term: His will was not at all to come
wordNotes.explanation: Paul strongly encouraged Apollos to visit, yet respected his judgment that the time was not right. The verse contradicts the idea that Paul and Apollos were competing factional leaders. Their partnership allowed persuasion without coercion and room for differing schedules.

verses.bibleText: Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Four urgent commands call the Corinthians to spiritual maturity: be watchful, stand firm in the faith, act courageously, and be strong. These are not unrelated exhortations. Together they describe a congregation that remains alert, rooted, brave, and dependent upon God.

Watchfulness is spiritual attentiveness, not anxious suspicion. Paul does not ask believers to imagine enemies everywhere or become obsessed with danger. He calls them to remain awake to the threats already exposed throughout the letter: division, pride, immorality, idolatry, misuse of freedom, disorder in worship, and denial of resurrection. A careless church can drift while still appearing active.

To “stand firm in the faith” means remaining loyal to the gospel they received. The command carries particular force after chapter 15. Christ truly died, was buried, rose, and will raise His people. The Corinthians must not be moved away from that hope by fashionable reasoning or spiritual overconfidence. Their firmness rests not in stubborn personality but in the truth of Christ.

The expression traditionally translated “act like men” is an idiom calling for mature courage. It is addressed to the entire congregation, women and men alike. Paul is not defining courage as exclusively masculine or suggesting that women lack bravery. He summons every believer to leave childish instability and face spiritual responsibility with steadfastness.

“Be strong” does not authorize aggression, domination, or harshness. Christian strength is not the ability to intimidate others or force one’s will upon the church. Paul has repeatedly overturned worldly ideas of power. The strength believers need comes from God and is displayed in fidelity, endurance, self-control, and willingness to serve.

wordNotes.explanation: The older expression means to act courageously and maturely. Paul addresses the whole church, not male dominance, calling every believer to vigilance, firmness in the faith, and Spirit-given strength. The courage commanded in this verse is immediately governed by love.

verses.bibleText: Let all your things be done with charity.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Let all that you do be done in love” governs every command that precedes it. Watchfulness, firmness, courage, and strength can all become distorted unless love directs them. Paul does not allow the Corinthians to choose between conviction and compassion. Mature faith requires both.

The word “all” reaches back across the entire letter. Love must govern how believers respond to division. It must shape discipline so that holiness is protected without cruelty. It must guide knowledge, preventing those who understand their freedom from wounding a weaker conscience. It must transform worship so that gatherings build up the whole body rather than display individual gifts.

Love must govern the Lord’s Supper, where the wealthy had ignored others. It must shape the exercise of spiritual gifts, giving priority to what strengthens the congregation. It must direct leadership, financial giving, hospitality, disagreement, correction, and the treatment of workers such as Timothy and Apollos.

This love is not sentimental approval of everything people do. Earlier chapters have shown that love can warn, confront, and call for repentance. To love someone is not to leave that person undisturbed in conduct that destroys faith or harms others. Yet correction must seek restoration rather than humiliation.

Love gives Christian strength its proper form. It refuses to dominate the weak, flaunt liberty, demand status, or turn gifted people into rivals. It is patient, kind, unselfish, truthful, enduring, and hopeful. The portrait given in chapter 13 now becomes a final practical command. The Corinthians do not need courage that makes them severe or firmness that makes them unteachable. They need a love strong enough to remain holy, humble enough to serve, and brave enough to seek another person’s good at personal cost.

Everything done in love will not necessarily be received without conflict. Yet love ensures that the motive, manner, and goal remain shaped by Christ rather than by pride. It is the atmosphere in which every Christian gift and responsibility is meant to function.

wordNotes.explanation: Love is to govern “all” the church does, including watchfulness, firmness, courage, and strength from the previous verse. Paul does not oppose conviction and tenderness. Christian strength becomes faithful only when it seeks another person’s genuine good.

verses.bibleText: I beseech you, brethren, (ye know the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and that they have addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints,)

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul directs attention to the household of Stephanas, whom the Corinthians know personally. They were “the firstfruits of Achaia,” an early result of the gospel’s entrance into that region. Like the first portion of a harvest, their conversion signaled that God was beginning a larger work. Being first did not automatically make them worthy of influence. Paul commends what their lives had become: they “devoted themselves to the service of the saints.” Their standing rests not merely upon chronology, family name, or formal position, but upon observable service.

The whole household is mentioned without details about each member. Paul does not invite speculation about their precise responsibilities. What matters is their shared devotion. They had seen needs among believers and willingly placed themselves at the church’s service.

The phrase “devoted themselves” suggests deliberate commitment. Service did not happen only when convenient or when public recognition was guaranteed. They made care for God’s people a settled part of their life. This kind of ministry reflects the teaching of the entire letter. The Corinthians had often pursued status, giftedness, and public prominence. The household of Stephanas displayed another form of greatness: dependable labor for others. Their authority arose through service rather than self-promotion.

“Service of the saints” may include many practical and spiritual forms of care, but Paul does not provide a list. The absence of detail keeps attention on the disposition itself. They were people upon whom the church could rely.

The term “saints” once again dignifies those served. Christian ministry is not a powerful person bestowing favors upon inferior recipients. It is one member of Christ’s body caring for another. Stephanas and his household did not make the church their possession; they gave themselves for its good. Quiet devotion may receive less attention than visible gifts, but it is precious in the life of the church. The household of Stephanas had become a living answer to the divisions at Corinth: instead of gathering people around themselves, they had given themselves to serving God’s people.

wordNotes.explanation: The KJV phrase means they devoted or appointed themselves to serving the saints, not that they were controlled by a harmful compulsion. Stephanas’s household became recognizable leaders through sustained, voluntary service rather than through self-promoting claims to rank.

verses.bibleText: That ye submit yourselves unto such, and to every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The Corinthians are urged to “submit to such people and to everyone who joins in the work and labors.” Submission here must be understood in the light of the people being commended. They are not self-exalting rulers demanding control; they are servants who have devoted themselves to the good of the saints.

Appropriate submission includes cooperation, respect, teachability, and willingness to support faithful labor. A congregation should not make ministry unnecessarily difficult by resisting every responsible effort, despising guidance, or refusing to work alongside proven servants.

The words “such people” are important. The Corinthians are to respond appropriately to those whose lives demonstrate devotion to the church. Influence is connected with tested character and labor. Paul does not say, “Submit to anyone who claims authority.” He widens the instruction to include “everyone who joins in the work and labors.” Ministry is larger than one household or a small leadership circle. All who faithfully cooperate in the gospel deserve respect and support. The church should recognize work that may be strenuous, hidden, and easily taken for granted.

Submission in this context helps overcome factionalism. A divided congregation may resist leaders associated with another group, cooperate only with preferred personalities, or treat every direction as a contest for power. Paul calls them to value faithful labor rather than partisan identity.

Teachability does not mean surrendering discernment. Believers may ask whether a leader’s teaching is true, conduct is holy, and decisions genuinely serve the church. Yet discernment differs from habitual suspicion and prideful resistance. The Corinthians had admired impressive speech and conspicuous gifts. Paul asks them to honor something more enduring: people who work hard in love. Submission to such workers means making room for faithful service to bear fruit.

wordNotes.explanation: Submission here recognizes those whose labor faithfully serves the church. It is not blind obedience or immunity from accountability. The congregation should cooperate with proven servants and fellow workers whose lives demonstrate costly commitment to the gospel community.

verses.bibleText: I am glad of the coming of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus: for that which was lacking on your part they have supplied.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul rejoices at the arrival of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus. Their presence has brought him something the distant Corinthians could not personally provide. His words need not be read as an accusation that the congregation had deliberately neglected him. Physical separation created a real absence, and these three believers helped bridge it.

They carried the fellowship of Corinth into Paul’s present circumstances. Through their arrival, he was no longer dealing only with reports, questions, and problems from afar. He could encounter members of the congregation face to face and experience their companionship. “What was lacking on your part” can be understood as what distance prevented the whole congregation from supplying. The three did not replace the Corinthians or prove that the rest were useless. They represented something of the church’s care and made fellowship tangible where the entire community could not be present.

This highlights the spiritual value of personal presence. Messages and gifts can cross distances, but a faithful person carries encouragement in a distinctive way. Listening, praying, speaking, and simply sharing the burden of another can provide what written communication cannot.

Paul is not embarrassed to say that he benefited from their arrival. Apostles and leaders also need companionship. Spiritual strength does not eliminate the need to be refreshed through other believers. The church serves its servants as well as receiving service from them. The naming of all three men honors shared ministry. Stephanas has received special attention, but Fortunatus and Achaicus are not invisible. Paul remembers those whose work might otherwise have gone unmentioned.

Paul’s joy teaches the Corinthians to value what these men have done. The church may be tempted to celebrate only dramatic gifts, but dependable people who show up, carry responsibility, and strengthen relationships make an essential contribution.

Through these three servants, the absent Corinthians became near. Their presence did not solve every problem, but it refreshed the bond between Paul and the congregation. Sometimes one of the most spiritual things a believer can offer is the gift of faithful presence.

wordNotes.explanation: Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus supplied the personal presence the distant congregation could not provide. The phrase is affectionate rather than accusatory. Their visit carried fellowship across distance and allowed these representatives to serve both Paul and the Corinthians.

verses.bibleText: For they have refreshed my spirit and your's: therefore acknowledge ye them that are such.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus “refreshed” Paul’s spirit and the spirit of the Corinthians. Refreshment suggests relief, renewal, and encouragement. Their ministry did not merely complete an assignment; it strengthened people inwardly. Paul had carried concern for Corinth through much of the letter. The arrival of faithful believers from that congregation could assure him that grace was still active among them. Their presence brought companionship and perhaps a clearer sense of connection with the church he loved.

They also refreshed the Corinthians. By serving Paul faithfully, strengthening communication, and returning with his counsel and affection, they contributed to the congregation’s well-being. Their ministry moved in both directions. They did not build importance for themselves by keeping Paul and the church dependent upon them.

Paul commands, “Give recognition to such people.” Recognition is not celebrity. It is the honest acknowledgment of faithful character and labor. The church should know whom it can trust, appreciate those who serve, and refuse to let dependable work pass unnoticed. Some ministries are highly visible; others operate quietly. Paul’s words protect quiet workers from being forgotten. People who encourage, carry burdens, maintain relationships, and provide practical help may not appear impressive, but they can refresh the spirit of an entire community.

Recognition also serves discernment. The Corinthians had sometimes admired the wrong qualities—rhetorical display, status, knowledge, or conspicuous giftedness. Paul directs their attention toward servants whose lives produce peace, strength, and fellowship.

To recognize such people does not mean treating them as flawless. Appreciation need not become uncritical devotion. It means allowing proven faithfulness to carry appropriate weight and expressing gratitude rather than taking labor for granted. Churches can exhaust faithful workers when they demand much and recognize little. Paul calls for a different culture—one in which service is noticed, gratitude is spoken, and encouragement is shared.

Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus are honored not for commanding attention but for bringing renewal. Their example invites believers to ask not how visible their role is, but whether their presence refreshes others. In a divided and troubled church, people who quietly restore strength and connection are gifts worthy of grateful recognition.

wordNotes.explanation: These coworkers brought encouragement that renewed Paul inwardly and would also benefit the congregation they represented. Refreshing ministry may be quiet—presence, news, practical help, loyal friendship—yet Paul asks the church to recognize people whose service produces such life-giving strength.

verses.bibleText: The churches of Asia salute you. Aquila and Priscilla salute you much in the Lord, with the church that is in their house.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The greetings now widen beyond Paul’s immediate circle. “The churches of Asia” send their affection to Corinth. The Corinthians are reminded that they belong to a fellowship much larger than their own congregation. Other communities know of them, care about them, and share the same Lord.

This wider belonging corrects local self-importance. The Corinthian church cannot shape Christianity solely according to its preferences or behave as though its gifts and debates are the whole of God’s work. It stands among many churches that confess Christ and depend upon the same grace.

Aquila and Prisca send especially warm greetings “in the Lord,” together with the church that gathers in their house. Their home has become a place where believers meet as a congregation. The emphasis is not upon a sacred building but upon a community of people joined in Christ. Their greeting is rooted “in the Lord.” Christian fellowship is more than social friendliness or shared history. The Lord Jesus creates the relationship. People from different places can regard one another as family because they belong to Him.

The mention of a congregation meeting in a home also shows how hospitality can serve the gospel. Space, possessions, and ordinary household life can be opened for the strengthening of believers. Paul does not reveal the details of their gatherings, so nothing should be invented. The simple fact is enough: a home has become a center of Christian fellowship.

Aquila and Prisca are named together as participants in this ministry. Paul does not present one as spiritually significant and the other as incidental. Their shared greeting and the congregation associated with their house display cooperative service. For the Corinthians, these greetings are more than polite additions at the end of a letter. They remind a divided church that believers elsewhere regard them with affection. Their problems have not removed them from the wider family, but neither are they free to live without regard for that family’s shared faith.

wordNotes.term: The church that is in their house
wordNotes.explanation: Before dedicated church buildings became common, believers often gathered in homes such as that of Aquila and Priscilla. A household could become a center of worship, teaching, hospitality, and mission. “Church” names the gathered people, not the architecture around them.

verses.bibleText: All the brethren greet you. Greet ye one another with an holy kiss.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“All the brothers and sisters greet you.” The affection is not limited to prominent leaders. The whole believing community surrounding Paul extends fellowship toward Corinth. The Corinthians are being embraced by people beyond their immediate disputes.

Paul then tells them to greet one another “with a holy kiss.” The form expressed family-like affection and peace among believers. The adjective “holy” matters. The greeting is to be pure, respectful, and consistent with belonging to God. It must never become improper, coercive, or self-serving. The underlying command is not that every culture must reproduce the exact physical gesture without regard for local meaning. Forms of greeting differ, and concerns of consent, health, personal boundaries, and propriety must be honored. A physical expression that communicates holy affection in one setting may communicate discomfort or intrusion in another.

Yet the principle should not be weakened into a distant politeness. Paul calls believers to receive one another with sincere warmth. Christian fellowship is not meant to be cold, suspicious, or merely formal. Those reconciled to God are to express genuine reconciliation with one another.

This command carries special force in Corinth. The congregation had been divided by factions, status, wealth, knowledge, and differing loyalties. A holy greeting would require people who had separated themselves emotionally and socially to acknowledge one another as family in Christ. The greeting therefore becomes more than etiquette. It is a visible denial of hostility and exclusion. It says that the other believer is received not as a rival or inconvenience but as someone who belongs to the Lord.

“Holy” affection also respects truth. A greeting should not become a performance hiding unresolved abuse or unrepentant harm. Reconciliation may require confession, repentance, justice, and wise boundaries. Paul does not ask people to use an outward gesture to conceal what must be addressed.

wordNotes.explanation: The kiss was a culturally recognizable greeting of family affection. “Holy” distinguishes it from manipulation or impropriety and places ordinary welcome under Christ’s purity. The abiding command is sincere, peaceable recognition of fellow believers, expressed in culturally fitting ways.

verses.bibleText: The salutation of me Paul with mine own hand.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul now adds, “I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand.” The personal handwriting marks the conclusion as unmistakably his. Whatever assistance may have been involved in preparing the body of the letter, the final greeting carries Paul’s own written touch. This serves both authentication and affection. The Corinthians can recognize that the letter genuinely comes from the apostle who founded and loved their congregation. The strong correction throughout the letter is not the work of a distant impersonal authority hiding behind others. Paul places his own hand upon the closing words.

No conclusions about the condition of his eyesight, the size of his handwriting, or a particular illness should be built from this statement. Paul gives no such explanation here. The significance lies in the personal nature of the greeting.

A handwritten ending can also slow the reader down. After many chapters of carefully reasoned instruction, Paul steps forward personally. He is not merely a theologian solving problems. He is a pastor writing to people whose faith and future matter deeply to him. The autograph also carries responsibility. Paul stands behind what has been written. He does not distance himself from difficult commands or blame a secretary for the severity of the letter. The teaching, warnings, appeals, and expressions of hope bear his apostolic authority.

Christian communication can become detached, especially when difficult issues are addressed. Paul’s personal mark reminds us that truth should be spoken by people willing to own their words before God and remain relationally present to those addressed.

wordNotes.explanation: Paul customarily dictated letters through a scribe, then added a handwritten greeting as a personal and authenticating mark. The change of hand connected the apostle physically to the congregation and helped distinguish his communication from messages falsely attributed to him.

verses.bibleText: If any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s closing warning is severe: “If anyone has no love for the Lord, let him be accursed.” The issue is not ordinary emotional weakness, a struggling believer’s imperfect affection, or disagreement over a minor question. Paul is speaking about a settled absence of allegiance to Christ—a rejection of the Lord at the center of Christian faith.

Throughout the letter, the Corinthians have been called back to Christ crucified and risen. Every gift, freedom, doctrine, and hope depends upon Him. To remain unmoved toward the Lord while associating outwardly with His church is not a harmless deficiency. Christianity without love for Christ has lost its center.

The word translated “accursed” or “anathema” places the person under divine judgment. Paul is not giving believers permission to pronounce personal curses upon opponents, critics, or those with whom they disagree. The judgment belongs to God. Human beings must not use this verse to baptize anger, revenge, or factional hostility. Yet the warning is immediately joined to “Maranatha,” most naturally understood as the prayer, “Our Lord, come.” Judgment and hope meet in the expectation of Christ’s return. For those who reject Him, His coming is a solemn reality. For those who love Him, it is the fulfillment of resurrection hope.

Chapter 15 has just proclaimed that the dead will be raised, death will be defeated, and Christ’s people will receive imperishable life. “Our Lord, come” gathers that hope into a brief cry. The church does not merely remember that Jesus came; it longs for His appearing and the completion of His victory.

Love for Christ does not mean flawless emotion at every moment. It means belonging to Him, trusting Him, honoring His lordship, and continuing to turn toward Him in faith. Weak love may be strengthened; wandering believers may repent; grace can restore those who return. Maranatha, "Our Lord, come," expresses the church's Advent hope. The coming is the personal, visible return at which Christ raises the sleeping dead, transforms the living faithful, and brings His kingdom to completion. Love for Christ therefore includes longing for His appearing and living in readiness, not calculating a secret escape or abandoning present service.

wordNotes.explanation: “Anathema” pronounces judgment; “Maranatha” is an Aramaic appeal or confession that the Lord comes. Paul closes with the seriousness of allegiance to Jesus: persistent refusal to love the Lord faces His judgment. The phrase is not permission for Christians to curse personal opponents.

verses.bibleText: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you.” After a letter filled with rebuke, instruction, and demanding calls to holiness, grace is the appropriate final blessing. The Corinthians’ hope does not rest upon their ability to repair themselves through unaided effort.

Grace is not a conventional word placed at the end because Paul has nothing more to say. It is the active favor, mercy, presence, and power of Jesus Christ. The Lord who called the Corinthians is able to forgive them, sustain them, correct them, and continue transforming them. They need grace because the problems addressed in the letter cannot be solved merely through stronger resolutions. Division requires hearts humbled by the cross. Sexual impurity requires cleansing and renewed allegiance. Pride in knowledge must be transformed into love. Disorder in worship must give way to concern for the body. Doubt about resurrection must be met by the power of the risen Christ.

The blessing is “of the Lord Jesus.” Grace has a personal source. It comes through the One who gave Himself, was raised, and reigns as Lord. The Corinthians are not invited to depend upon a vague spiritual influence but upon Christ Himself.

“Be with you” expresses continuing presence. Paul may be absent, Timothy may come and go, Apollos may delay, and travel plans may change, but the grace of Christ can remain with the congregation. Their deepest security does not depend upon the constant physical presence of any human leader. The congregation began through grace and must continue through grace. Watchfulness, firmness, courage, strength, generosity, service, and love are all responses enabled by the Lord’s favor. Paul leaves them not with confidence in their own maturity, but with confidence in Jesus Christ, whose grace is sufficient to preserve and renew His people.

wordNotes.term: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ
wordNotes.explanation: Grace has the final word after a letter containing severe correction. Paul asks that Christ’s unearned favor and sustaining presence remain with the congregation. The blessing reminds readers that obedience, restoration, and perseverance all depend upon the Lord who gives before He commands.

verses.bibleText: My love be with you all in Christ Jesus. Amen.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul’s final words are intensely personal: “My love be with you all in Christ Jesus.” The letter has contained some of the strongest correction imaginable, yet Paul does not end in anger or emotional withdrawal. He affirms love for the entire congregation. “All” is significant. His affection is not reserved for the members who agreed with him, welcomed his authority, or behaved faithfully. It extends across the divided church—to the strong and the weak, the gifted and the overlooked, the repentant and those still needing to repent.

His love is “in Christ Jesus.” Christ is its source, character, and boundary. Paul does not love them merely because the relationship is easy or because they reward his labor. He loves them as people joined to him through the Lord who loved them both.

Love in Christ is stronger than wounded pride. Paul has been misunderstood, his authority has been questioned, and his work has often been complicated by their behavior. Yet he refuses to let pain have the final word. He remains committed to their good. The phrase “be with you” gives his love an enduring quality. Though physically absent, Paul wants the Corinthians to know that his affection accompanies the letter. The rebukes should not be read as rejection. The hand that corrected them remains extended toward them in fellowship.

wordNotes.term: My love be with you all
wordNotes.explanation: Paul’s love extends to the entire troubled congregation, including those he has corrected sharply. “In Christ Jesus” defines this affection as covenant loyalty rather than sentiment. Truthful rebuke and enduring pastoral love have belonged together throughout the letter.
